./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there is only one example - this request is equivalent to the regular search with vector of that
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there are more than one example, Qdrant will attempt to search for similar to all of them.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                Negative examples is an experimental functionality.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                Strategy defines how to combine multiple examples into a recommendation query.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'average_vector' - calculates average vector of all examples and uses it for search
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'best_score' - finds the result which is closer to positive examples and further from negative

./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. ./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for points based on target and/or positive and negative example pairs, in batch.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Use context and a target to find the most similar points to the target, constrained by the context. When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. This means that the score minimizes the scenario of finding a point closer to a negative than to a positive part of a pair. Since the score of a context relates to loss, the maximum score a point can get is 0.0, and it becomes normal that many points can have a score of 0.0. When using target (with or without context), the score behaves a little different: The  integer part of the score represents the rank with respect to the context, while the decimal part of the score relates to the distance to the target. The context part of the score for  each pair is calculated +1 if the point is closer to a positive than to a negative part of a pair,  and -1 otherwise.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples.

./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    Recommendation request. Provides positive and negative examples of the vectors, which can be ids of points that are already stored in the collection, raw vectors, or even ids and vectors combined.  Service should look for the points which are closer to positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples. The concrete way of how to compare negative and positive distances is up to the `strategy` chosen.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    positive: Optional[List["RecommendExample"]] = Field(default=[], description="Look for vectors closest to those")
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    negative: Optional[List["RecommendExample"]] = Field(default=[], description="Try to avoid vectors like this")
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:        default=None, description="How to use positive and negative examples to find the results"
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    How to use positive and negative examples to find the results, default is `average_vector`:  * `average_vector` - Average positive and negative vectors and create a single query with the formula `query = avg_pos + avg_pos - avg_neg`. Then performs normal search.  * `best_score` - Uses custom search objective. Each candidate is compared against all examples, its score is then chosen from the `max(max_pos_score, max_neg_score)`. If the `max_neg_score` is chosen then it is squared and negated, otherwise it is just the `max_pos_score`.
./backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:        description="Examples: - URL `http://localhost:8080/collections/my_collection/snapshots/my_snapshot` - Local path `file:///qdrant/snapshots/test_collection-2022-08-04-10-49-10.snapshot`",

./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for points based on target and/or positive and negative example pairs, in batch.
./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Use context and a target to find the most similar points to the target, constrained by the context. When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. This means that the score minimizes the scenario of finding a point closer to a negative than to a positive part of a pair. Since the score of a context relates to loss, the maximum score a point can get is 0.0, and it becomes normal that many points can have a score of 0.0. When using target (with or without context), the score behaves a little different: The  integer part of the score represents the rank with respect to the context, while the decimal part of the score relates to the distance to the target. The context part of the score for  each pair is calculated +1 if the point is closer to a positive than to a negative part of a pair,  and -1 otherwise.
./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples.
./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples, grouped by a given payload field.

./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    Recommendation request. Provides positive and negative examples of the vectors, which can be ids of points that are already stored in the collection, raw vectors, or even ids and vectors combined.  Service should look for the points which are closer to positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples. The concrete way of how to compare negative and positive distances is up to the `strategy` chosen.

./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:        default=None, description="How to use positive and negative examples to find the results"
./backend/.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    How to use positive and negative examples to find the results, default is `average_vector`:  * `average_vector` - Average positive and negative vectors and create a single query with the formula `query = avg_pos + avg_pos - avg_neg`. Then performs normal search.  * `best_score` - Uses custom search objective. Each candidate is compared against all examples, its score is then chosen from the `max(max_pos_score, max_neg_score)`. If the `max_neg_score` is chosen then it is squared and negated, otherwise it is just the `max_pos_score`.

./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:        """Recommend points: search for similar points based on already stored in Qdrant examples.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there is only one example - this request is equivalent to the regular search with vector of that
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there are more than one example, Qdrant will attempt to search for similar to all of them.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                Negative examples is an experimental functionality.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                Strategy defines how to combine multiple examples into a recommendation query.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'average_vector' - calculates average vector of all examples and uses it for search
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'best_score' - finds the result which is closer to positive examples and further from negative
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:        positive: Optional[Sequence[types.RecommendExample]] = None,

./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:        """Recommend point groups: search for similar points based on already stored in Qdrant examples
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:        Recommend best matches for given stored examples grouped by the value of payload field.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there is only one example - this request is equivalent to the regular search with vector of that
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                If there are more than one example, Qdrant will attempt to search for similar to all of them.

./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'average_vector' - calculates average vector of all examples and uses it for search
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                - 'best_score' - finds the result which is closer to positive examples and further from negative
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:        context: Optional[Sequence[types.ContextExamplePair]] = None,
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                Pairs of { positive, negative } examples to constrain the search.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/async_qdrant_client.py:                When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. This means that the score minimizes the scenario of finding a point closer to a negative than to a positive part of a pair.

./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for points based on target and/or positive and negative example pairs, in batch.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Use context and a target to find the most similar points to the target, constrained by the context. When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. This means that the score minimizes the scenario of finding a point closer to a negative than to a positive part of a pair. Since the score of a context relates to loss, the maximum score a point can get is 0.0, and it becomes normal that many points can have a score of 0.0. When using target (with or without context), the score behaves a little different: The  integer part of the score represents the rank with respect to the context, while the decimal part of the score relates to the distance to the target. The context part of the score for  each pair is calculated +1 if the point is closer to a positive than to a negative part of a pair,  and -1 otherwise.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples, grouped by a given payload field.

./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    Recommendation request. Provides positive and negative examples of the vectors, which can be ids of points that are already stored in the collection, raw vectors, or even ids and vectors combined.  Service should look for the points which are closer to positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples. The concrete way of how to compare negative and positive distances is up to the `strategy` chosen.
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    positive: Optional[List["RecommendExample"]] = Field(default=[], description="Look for vectors closest to those")
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    negative: Optional[List["RecommendExample"]] = Field(default=[], description="Try to avoid vectors like this")
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:        default=None, description="How to use positive and negative examples to find the results"
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    How to use positive and negative examples to find the results, default is `average_vector`:  * `average_vector` - Average positive and negative vectors and create a single query with the formula `query = avg_pos + avg_pos - avg_neg`. Then performs normal search.  * `best_score` - Uses custom search objective. Each candidate is compared against all examples, its score is then chosen from the `max(max_pos_score, max_neg_score)`. If the `max_neg_score` is chosen then it is squared and negated, otherwise it is just the `max_pos_score`.

./.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for points based on target and/or positive and negative example pairs, in batch.
./.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Use context and a target to find the most similar points to the target, constrained by the context. When using only the context (without a target), a special search - called context search - is performed where pairs of points are used to generate a loss that guides the search towards the zone where most positive examples overlap. This means that the score minimizes the scenario of finding a point closer to a negative than to a positive part of a pair. Since the score of a context relates to loss, the maximum score a point can get is 0.0, and it becomes normal that many points can have a score of 0.0. When using target (with or without context), the score behaves a little different: The  integer part of the score represents the rank with respect to the context, while the decimal part of the score relates to the distance to the target. The context part of the score for  each pair is calculated +1 if the point is closer to a positive than to a negative part of a pair,  and -1 otherwise.
./.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/api/points_api.py:        Look for the points which are closer to stored positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples.

./.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    Recommendation request. Provides positive and negative examples of the vectors, which can be ids of points that are already stored in the collection, raw vectors, or even ids and vectors combined.  Service should look for the points which are closer to positive examples and at the same time further to negative examples. The concrete way of how to compare negative and positive distances is up to the `strategy` chosen.

./.venv/lib64/python3.12/site-packages/qdrant_client/http/models/models.py:    How to use positive and negative examples to find the results, default is `average_vector`:  * `average_vector` - Average positive and negative vectors and create a single query with the formula `query = avg_pos + avg_pos - avg_neg`. Then performs normal search.  * `best_score` - Uses custom search objective. Each candidate is compared against all examples, its score is then chosen from the `max(max_pos_score, max_neg_score)`. If the `max_neg_score` is chosen then it is squared and negated, otherwise it is just the `max_pos_score`.
